serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one Bombay Sandwich comprising two slices of white bread with vegetable filling and green chutney, weighing approximately 180 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Sandwich (vegetable, with bread)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Bombay Sandwich as a discrete entry. Values are estimated by component analysis using ICMR-NIN data for white bread, boiled potato, cucumber, tomato, onion, butter, and green chutney in proportions consistent with a standard Bombay Sandwich recipe (approximately 40% bread, 30% potato, 15% vegetables, 8% chutney, 7% butter/condiments). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread and mixed vegetable sandwich analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bombay Sandwich. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white bread (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the presence of boiled potato, vegetables, green chutney, and butter, which lower the overall glycemic response. A composite GI of 65 (medium) is defensible per published mixed-sandwich analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 58.5) / 100 = 38.1, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 32.5 × 1.8 = 58.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~756 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bombay Sandwich consumption due to moderate-to-high sodium from butter, chaat masala, and salt; one serving provides approximately 33% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (65) and high glycemic load per serving (~38)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Bombay Sandwich in moderation; the medium GI of 65 and high glycemic load of approximately 38 per serving can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise, and portion control or substitution with wholegrain bread is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~3.5 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat contribution from butter and the moderate sodium load warrant portion control; replacing butter with a plant-based spread and reducing added salt can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this sandwich.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bombay Sandwich (also known as Mumbai Sandwich) is a popular Indian street food originating from Mumbai (formerly Bombay), India. It consists of sliced white bread generously spread with green chutney made from fresh coriander, mint, and green chilli, layered with thinly sliced boiled potato, cucumber, tomato, and onion, then seasoned with chaat masala and cumin powder before being buttered and optionally grilled or toasted. The result is a sandwich with a vibrant, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile, with a soft interior and a lightly crisp exterior when toasted. The green chutney provides a herbaceous freshness that is the defining flavour of this dish. Nutritionally, Bombay Sandwich is a moderate-energy food (~218 kcal per 100 g) with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient from bread and potato, moderate fat from butter, and modest protein. It contains meaningful dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g) from vegetables and bread. Sodium is moderately elevated due to chaat masala, butter, and added salt, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~65) and high glycemic load per serving mean that people with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control. In Singapore, Bombay Sandwich is served at Indian restaurants and cafes as a snack or light meal, often priced as a premium item. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Bombay Sandwich?
What is in a Bombay Sandwich?
What is the difference between Bombay Sandwich and Mumbai Sandwich?
What chutney is used in Bombay Sandwich?

confidence_reason: No discrete Bombay Sandwich entry exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are derived from a component-level estimate using ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients in standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread and mixed vegetable sandwich data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients; no single approved source entry for Bombay Sandwich exists. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based and dairy-butter sandwich with no animal flesh or fermented products; confirmed zero by ingredient composition — naturally zero for this vegetarian formulation. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category and the ingredient composition does not include significant vitamin D sources. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Bombay Sandwich has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded; Bombay Sandwich is a vegetarian dish with no relation to Butter Chicken. 7. The restaurant 'Dugout - Circular Road' serves this item at SGD 15.00, which is consistent with a premium cafe-style Indian sandwich in Singapore's CBD area.
